            if (result != null) {

            }

    return null;
}

But in the initial approach, the code doesn't use memoization, which might lead to redundant processing.

So, to optimize, we can add memoization.

Thus, the code outline becomes:

- Precompute the necessary info for each number.

- Sort the list.

- Use a recursive function with memoization to track whether a state can lead to a solution.

- For each state, if it's already in the memo, return the stored result.

- Otherwise, process the state, store the result in the memo, and return it.

This way, each state is processed only once.

So, the code would look something like this:

class Solution {
    class NumberInfo {
        int num;
        int m;
        int numMod;
        int pow10Mod;

        NumberInfo(int num, int m, int numMod, int pow10Mod) {
            this.num = num;
            this.m = m;
            this.numMod = numMod;
            this.pow10Mod = pow10Mod;
        }
    }

    public List<Integer> solve(List<Integer> nums, int k) {
        int n = nums.size();
        List<NumberInfo> list = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int num : nums) {
            int m = String.valueOf(num).length();
            int numMod = num % k;
            int pow10Mod = 1;
            for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
                pow10Mod = (pow10Mod * 10) % k;
            }
            list.add(new NumberInfo(num, m, numMod, pow10Mod));
        }
        Collections.sort(list, Comparator.comparingInt(ni -> ni.num));

        Map<Triple<Integer, Integer, Integer>, Boolean> memo = new HashMap<>();
        List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
        if (backtrack(0, 0, 1, 0, new ArrayList<>(), list, k, memo, result)) {
            return result;
        } else {
            return new ArrayList<>();
        }
    }

    private boolean backtrack(int index, int currentRemainder, int currentPower, int usedMask, List<Integer> path, List<NumberInfo> list, int k, Map<Triple<Integer, Integer, Integer>, Boolean> memo, List<Integer> result) {
        Triple<Integer, Integer, Integer> state = new Triple<>(currentRemainder, currentPower, usedMask);
        if (memo.containsKey(state)) {
            return memo.get(state);
        }

        if (index == list.size()) {
            if (currentRemainder == 0) {
                result.addAll(path);
                memo.put(state, true);
                return true;
            } else {
                memo.put(state, false);
                return false;
            }
        }

        for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++) {
            if ((usedMask & (1 << i)) == 0) {
                NumberInfo info = list.get(i);
                int newRemainder = (currentRemainder * info.pow10Mod + info.numMod) % k;
                if (newRemainder < 0) newRemainder += k; // Ensure it's positive
                int newPower = (currentPower * info.pow10Mod) % k;
                int newUsedMask = usedMask | (1 << i);
                List<Integer> newPath = new ArrayList<>(path);
                newPath.add(info.num);
                if (backtrack(index + 1, newRemainder, newPower, newUsedMask, newPath, list, k, memo, result)) {
                    memo.put(state, true);
                    return true;
                }
            }
        }

        memo.put(state, false);
        return false;
    }

    class Triple<A, B, C> {
        A a;
        B b;
        C c;

        Triple(A a, B b, C c) {
            this.a = a;
            this.b = b;
            this.c = c;
        }

        @Override
        public boolean equals(Object o) {
            if (this == o) return true;
            if (o == null || getClass() != o.getClass()) return false;
            Triple<?, ?, ?> triple = (Triple<?, ?, ?>) o;
            return a.equals(triple.a) && b.equals(triple.b) && c.equals(triple.c);
        }

        @Override
        public int hashCode() {
            return Objects.hash(a, b, c);
        }
    }
}

Wait, but in the code above, the 'result' is being passed as a parameter, and when a solution is found, it's added to the result. However, in the initial call, the result is an empty list, and when the backtrack function finds a solution, it adds the path to the result.

But in the code, the 'path' is built as a new list each time, which could be inefficient. Alternatively, the function could build the permutation by backtracking and constructing it once a solution is found.

But for the sake of time, this approach should work.

Now, considering the efficiency:

- The memoization reduces the number of states processed.

- The function tries numbers in sorted order, ensuring the first solution found is the lex smallest.

- The time complexity is O(k^2 * 2^n), which for n=13 and k=100 is manageable.

Thus, the code should work efficiently.
</think>

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    class NumberInfo {
        int num;
        int m;
        int numMod;
        int pow10Mod;

        NumberInfo(int num, int m, int numMod, int pow10Mod) {
            this.num = num;
            this.m = m;
            this.numMod = numMod;
            this.pow10Mod = pow10Mod;
        }
    }

    public List<Integer> solve(List<Integer> nums, int k) {
        int n = nums.size();
        List<NumberInfo> list = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int num : nums) {
            int m = String.valueOf(num).length();
            int numMod = num % k;
            int pow10Mod = 1;
            for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
                pow10Mod = (pow10Mod * 10) % k;
            }
            list.add(new NumberInfo(num, m, numMod, pow10Mod));
        }
        Collections.sort(list, Comparator.comparingInt(ni -> ni.num));

        Map<Triple<Integer, Integer, Integer>, Boolean> memo = new HashMap<>();
        List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
        if (backtrack(0, 0, 1, 0, new ArrayList<>(), list, k, memo, result)) {
            return result;
        } else {
            return new ArrayList<>();
        }
    }

    private boolean backtrack(int index, int currentRemainder, int currentPower, int usedMask, List<Integer> path, List<NumberInfo> list, int k, Map<Triple<Integer, Integer, Integer>, Boolean> memo, List<Integer> result) {
        Triple<Integer, Integer, Integer> state = new Triple<>(currentRemainder, currentPower, usedMask);
        if (memo.containsKey(state)) {
            return memo.get(state);
        }

        if (index == list.size()) {
            if (currentRemainder == 0) {
                result.addAll(path);
                memo.put(state, true);
                return true;
            } else {
                memo.put(state, false);
                return false;
            }
        }

        for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++) {
            if ((usedMask & (1 << i)) == 0) {
                NumberInfo info = list.get(i);
                int newRemainder = (currentRemainder * info.pow10Mod + info.numMod) % k;
                if (newRemainder < 0) newRemainder += k;
                int newPower = (currentPower * info.pow10Mod) % k;
                int newUsedMask = usedMask | (1 << i);
                List<Integer> newPath = new ArrayList<>(path);
                newPath.add(info.num);
                if (backtrack(index + 1, newRemainder, newPower, newUsedMask, newPath, list, k, memo, result)) {
                    memo.put(state, true);
                    return true;
                }
            }
        }

        memo.put(state, false);
        return false;
    }

    class Triple<A, B, C> {
        A a;
        B b;
        C c;

        Triple(A a, B b, C c) {
            this.a = a;
            this.b = b;
            this.c = c;
        }

        @Override
        public boolean equals(Object o) {
            if (this == o) return true;
            if (o == null || getClass() != o.getClass()) return false;
            Triple<?, ?, ?> triple = (Triple<?, ?, ?>) o;
            return a.equals(triple.a) && b.equals(triple.b) && c.equals(triple.c);
        }

        @Override
        public int hashCode() {
            return Objects.hash(a, b, c);
        }
    }
}
```

efficiency: HIGH

time complexity: O(k^2 * 2^n)

space complexity: O(k^2 * 2^n)

energy implications: LOW

explanation: The solution uses a backtracking approach with memoization to efficiently explore possible permutations. By precomputing necessary values and leveraging memoization to avoid redundant state processing, it ensures that the lexicographically smallest valid permutation is found quickly. The approach efficiently handles the constraints and provides an optimal solution.
